question_text: Dry ice (solid carbon dioxide) at -78°C can change directly into carbon dioxide gas without passing through a liquid state. What is the name of this process, and why is it possible for carbon dioxide but unusual for water?

options.label: A
options.text: Condensation; because carbon dioxide is colder than water

options.label: B
options.text: Sublimation; because the atmospheric pressure is low enough that the solid can turn directly into gas without melting

options.label: C
options.text: Evaporation; because carbon dioxide molecules are smaller than water molecules

options.label: D
options.text: Freezing; because carbon dioxide cannot exist as a liquid at normal atmospheric pressure

correct_answer: Sublimation; because the atmospheric pressure is low enough that the solid can turn directly into gas without melting
explanation: Sublimation is when a solid changes directly into a gas without becoming a liquid first. This happens to dry ice (solid CO₂) at normal atmospheric pressure because CO₂'s triple point (where solid, liquid, and gas can coexist) occurs at a pressure lower than Earth's atmosphere. For water, the triple point pressure is much lower, so liquid water normally forms between ice and steam at atmospheric pressure.
